title: Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA)
content: A Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA) is a legally binding contract that establishes a confidential relationship between parties. It ensures that sensitive information such as trade secrets, business strategies, or proprietary data is not disclosed. Key elements include definition of confidential information, exclusions (public domain, prior knowledge), obligations of the receiving party, duration (typically 2–5 years), permitted disclosures, return or destruction of data, and remedies such as injunctive relief. NDAs may be unilateral or mutual.

title: Employment Contract
content: An employment contract defines the legal relationship between employer and employee. It includes job responsibilities, compensation, benefits, working hours, termination conditions, and confidentiality obligations. Common clauses include non-compete, non-solicitation, intellectual property ownership, dispute resolution, and governing law. In India, employment is also governed by labor laws such as the Industrial Disputes Act.

title: Liability and Indemnity Clauses
content: Liability clauses limit the extent a party is responsible for damages, often capping financial exposure or excluding indirect losses. Indemnity clauses require one party to compensate another for losses arising from specific events such as breach, negligence, or third-party claims. Best practices include mutual indemnification and clearly defined liability caps. Red flags include unlimited liability and one-sided indemnity.

title: Termination Clause
content: A termination clause defines how a contract can be ended. It includes termination for convenience, termination for cause, notice periods, cure periods, and consequences such as payment obligations and return of materials. It also specifies which obligations survive termination, such as confidentiality and IP rights.

title: Jurisdiction and Governing Law
content: This clause specifies which laws apply to a contract and which courts or tribunals will handle disputes. It ensures clarity in legal proceedings and enforcement. In India, contracts are governed by the Indian Contract Act 1872. Dispute resolution options include litigation, arbitration, and mediation.

title: Force Majeure Clause
content: A force majeure clause excuses performance when unforeseen events beyond control occur, such as natural disasters, war, pandemics, or government actions. It typically includes definitions of events, notice requirements, duration, and termination rights if disruption continues.

title: Non-Compete Clause
content: A non-compete clause restricts a party from engaging in competing activities after a relationship ends. Enforceability varies by jurisdiction. In India, post-employment non-competes are generally void under Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act. Reasonableness depends on time, geography, and scope.

title: Arbitration Clause
content: An arbitration clause requires disputes to be resolved through arbitration instead of courts. Arbitration is typically faster, private, and less formal. Clauses usually specify seat, governing rules, and number of arbitrators.

title: Confidentiality Clause
content: A confidentiality clause protects sensitive information shared between parties. It defines what constitutes confidential information, obligations to protect it, and consequences of breach.

title: Intellectual Property Clause
content: This clause defines ownership of intellectual property created during a contract. It distinguishes between assignment and licensing, includes work-for-hire provisions, and may address background IP and moral rights. Clear drafting prevents disputes over ownership.

title: Payment Terms Clause
content: Payment terms specify how and when payments must be made. They include due dates, payment methods, penalties for late payment, and installment structures.

title: Breach of Contract
content: A breach occurs when a party fails to fulfill contractual obligations. Breaches may be minor or material. Remedies include damages, termination, or specific performance depending on severity.

title: Consideration in Contracts
content: Consideration refers to something of value exchanged between parties. It is essential for a legally enforceable contract and can include money, services, or promises.

title: Offer and Acceptance
content: A valid contract requires a clear offer and acceptance. Acceptance must match the offer exactly and be communicated effectively.

title: Capacity to Contract
content: Capacity refers to a party’s legal ability to enter into a contract. Parties must be of legal age, sound mind, and not disqualified by law.

title: Duress and Undue Influence
content: Contracts formed under coercion or manipulation may be invalid. Duress involves threats, while undue influence involves misuse of a position of power.

title: Remedies for Breach
content: Remedies include compensatory damages, rescission, specific performance, and sometimes punitive damages. The goal is to restore the injured party.

title: Contract Interpretation
content: Contract interpretation involves determining the meaning of terms using plain language, intent of parties, and surrounding circumstances.

title: Entire Agreement Clause
content: This clause states that the written contract represents the full agreement between parties, excluding prior discussions or understandings.

title: Specific Performance
content: Specific performance is a remedy where a court orders a party to fulfill contractual obligations instead of paying damages, often used when monetary compensation is insufficient.

title: Rescission of Contract
content: Rescission cancels a contract and restores parties to their original positions, often used in cases of fraud, misrepresentation, or mistake.

title: Misrepresentation
content: Misrepresentation occurs when a false statement induces a contract. It can be innocent, negligent, or fraudulent, with different remedies available.

title: Void vs Voidable Contracts
content: Void contracts are unenforceable from the start, while voidable contracts remain valid until one party chooses to void them due to defects like coercion or misrepresentation.

title: Liquidated Damages
content: Liquidated damages are pre-agreed amounts payable upon breach. They must represent a genuine estimate of loss and not function as a penalty.

title: Penalty Clause
content: Penalty clauses impose excessive charges for breach and are generally unenforceable if deemed punitive rather than compensatory.

title: Standard Form Contracts
content: Standard form contracts are pre-drafted agreements with little room for negotiation, often used in consumer transactions and raising fairness concerns.

title: Unconscionable Contracts
content: Unconscionable contracts are extremely one-sided agreements that courts may refuse to enforce due to unfairness or unequal bargaining power.

title: Agency Relationship
content: An agency relationship exists when an agent acts on behalf of a principal and can create legal obligations for them.

title: Partnership Agreements
content: A partnership agreement defines roles, profit sharing, decision-making, and dispute resolution among business partners.

title: Contract Risk Assessment Framework
content: Contract risk assessment identifies high, medium, and low-risk clauses. High-risk examples include unlimited liability, unilateral changes, automatic renewal, and lack of dispute resolution. It helps prioritize negotiation focus.

title: Digital Contracts and E-Signatures
content: Electronic contracts are legally valid in many jurisdictions. In India, they are governed by the Information Technology Act 2000. E-signatures range from simple to qualified digital signatures. Some documents still require physical signatures.

title: Data Protection and Privacy Clauses
content: These clauses regulate how personal data is collected, processed, and protected. Key elements include data roles, security measures, breach notification, and compliance with laws such as GDPR and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023.

title: Service Agreement
content: A service agreement governs the delivery of services. It includes scope, timelines, payment, IP ownership, confidentiality, termination, and independent contractor status.

title: Agentic AI and LangGraph Concepts
content: LangGraph is a framework for building stateful AI systems using nodes, edges, and shared state. It supports retrieval, tool usage, and evaluation flows. Common patterns include ReAct, planning, and reflection. It is often used with vector databases like ChromaDB.
